acceptableAlternatives: rotational kinetic energy equals half I omega squared
quizQuestion: What equation gives the rotational kinetic energy of an object with moment of inertia I spinning at angular velocity ω?
explanation: K_rot = ½Iω² is the rotational analog of K = ½mv². I plays the role of mass, ω plays the role of v. Units: (kg⋅m²)(rad/s)² = kg⋅m²/s² = J. For a rolling object, total KE = ½mv² + ½Iω² (translational + rotational). Objects with more mass at the rim (larger I) have more rotational KE for the same ω.
visualDescription: A spinning flywheel with I and ω labeled, and K_rot = ½Iω² shown

acceptableAlternatives: total KE of rolling object is translational plus rotational
quizQuestion: What equation gives the total kinetic energy of an object rolling without slipping?
explanation: K_total = ½mv² + ½Iω². Rolling objects have both translational KE (center of mass moving) and rotational KE (spinning about CM). For rolling without slipping: v_cm = Rω, so ω = v/R. Substituting: K = ½mv² + ½I(v/R)². A hollow cylinder (I = MR²) has more rotational KE than a solid cylinder (I = ½MR²) and rolls slower down a ramp.
visualDescription: A rolling cylinder with both v (translational) and ω (rotational) arrows labeled, showing both KE contributions

acceptableAlternatives: linear speed equals radius times angular speed for rolling
quizQuestion: What condition relates the center-of-mass velocity to the angular velocity for an object rolling without slipping?
explanation: v_cm = Rω (rolling without slipping). This means the contact point has zero velocity relative to the ground — no skidding. If v_cm > Rω, the wheel is sliding forward (spinning tires on ice). If v_cm < Rω, the wheel is sliding backward (braking skid). This constraint connects linear and rotational motion for rolling problems.
visualDescription: A wheel rolling without slipping with v_cm labeled at center and ω labeled for rotation, showing the contact point is momentarily at rest

acceptableAlternatives: work done by torque equals torque times angular displacement
quizQuestion: What equation gives the work done by a constant torque τ through an angular displacement Δθ?
explanation: W = τΔθ is the rotational analog of W = Fd. Work done by torque is positive when torque and angular displacement are in the same rotational direction. Units: (N⋅m)(rad) = N⋅m = J (radians are dimensionless). This connects to the work-energy theorem: W = ΔK_rot = Δ(½Iω²).
visualDescription: A wheel rotated by applied torque τ through angle Δθ with work W = τΔθ labeled

acceptableAlternatives: angular momentum equals moment of inertia times angular velocity
quizQuestion: What equation defines angular momentum L for a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis?
explanation: L = Iω is the rotational analog of p = mv. Angular momentum has units of kg⋅m²/s. Like linear momentum, angular momentum is conserved when there is no external torque. For a point mass: L = mvr. The direction of L follows the right-hand rule — curl fingers in direction of rotation, thumb points in direction of L.
visualDescription: A spinning wheel with I and ω labeled, and L = Iω shown with an axial arrow

acceptableAlternatives: angular impulse equals torque times time
quizQuestion: What equation gives the angular impulse, analogous to the linear impulse J = FΔt?
explanation: ΔL = τΔt is the rotational analog of J = FΔt = Δp. A torque applied over a time interval changes the angular momentum. This follows from Στ = Iα = I(Δω/Δt) → τΔt = IΔω = ΔL. The angular impulse equals the area under a τ-t graph, just as linear impulse equals area under an F-t graph.
visualDescription: A torque-time graph with shaded area labeled ΔL = τΔt

correctAnswer: Conservation of angular momentum: L_i = L_f when Στ_ext = 0
acceptableAlternatives: angular momentum conserved when no external torque
quizQuestion: What law explains why a figure skater spins faster when pulling in their arms?
explanation: Conservation of angular momentum: L = Iω = constant when Στ_external = 0. When the skater pulls in arms, I decreases (mass moves closer to axis) → ω must increase to keep L = Iω constant. This explains spinning divers tucking, pulsars spinning up, and gyroscopes maintaining orientation. L is always conserved for isolated systems.
visualDescription: A figure skater going from arms-out (large I, small ω) to arms-in (small I, large ω), with L constant
distractors: Conservation of kinetic energy for rotation

correctAnswer: Gravitational force provides centripetal force: Gm₁m₂/r² = mv²/r
acceptableAlternatives: gravity = centripetal force for orbiting satellites
quizQuestion: For an orbiting satellite, what equation expresses the balance between gravitational force and the centripetal force requirement?
explanation: GMm/r² = mv²/r → v = √(GM/r). Orbital speed depends on the central mass M and orbit radius r, NOT the satellite's mass m. Larger orbit radius → smaller orbital speed. Satellites in low orbit move faster than those in high orbit. This explains why geosynchronous satellites (24-hr period) must be at a specific altitude (~36,000 km).
visualDescription: A satellite in circular orbit with gravity arrow pointing toward Earth equaling the centripetal force requirement

correctAnswer: Kepler's Third Law: T² ∝ r³ (T²/r³ = constant)
acceptableAlternatives: period squared proportional to orbital radius cubed
quizQuestion: What law states that the square of a planet's orbital period is proportional to the cube of its average orbital radius?
explanation: Kepler's Third Law: T² = (4π²/GM) r³, or T²/r³ = 4π²/GM = constant for all planets orbiting the same star. If you double the orbital radius, the period increases by a factor of 2^(3/2) ≈ 2.83. This can be derived by combining GMm/r² = mv²/r with T = 2πr/v.
visualDescription: Graph of T² vs. r³ for planets in the solar system, showing a straight line
